lesson.menu_desc: Holding your ground & trading concessions: close a deal with first-conditional trades, negotiation connectors and a calm, firm tone — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 — Negotiating the Deal — Holding Your Ground & Trading Concessions
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 8 — Negotiating the Deal

listenings.text: Okay, let me think this through before I walk in. My target is a five percent price cut and a four-week lead time. My walk-away point is clear: if the lead time stays above six weeks, there is no deal, because our branches cannot wait that long. My strongest bargaining chip is volume. We are ready to commit to the full annual order, and that is worth real money to them. So the trade is simple. If they bring the lead time down to four weeks, we will lock in the volume for the whole year. The price is where I can be flexible, but only in exchange for something. I will not give a single concession for free. The one thing that is non-negotiable is the service guarantee. On that, I hold firm. Everything else, we can trade. If we hit a deadlock on price, I change the variable. I do not chase it.

Hi Victor, it is Marina. Before your supplier meeting tomorrow, here are three quick reminders. First, open by mapping the common ground. Name the things you both already agree on, so the room starts from yes, not from no. Second, and this is the big one, never give a concession without getting one back. Do not gift, trade. If you move on the contract length, you get movement on the price. Every give needs a take. Third, know your one non-negotiable and say it calmly. You do not need to raise your voice to hold firm; a quiet, steady no is stronger than an angry one. And if you reach a deadlock, do not keep pushing the same point. Change a variable instead. Offer a longer term, a faster payment, a bigger volume, anything that lets both sides move without losing face. Go in calm, trade well, and protect that one line you will not cross. You have got this.
